preFix.title: Pyrography on a wood round slice
preFix.subtitle: Burning a decorative panel design onto a cross-cut wood round
preFix.excerpt: A cross-cut slice from a birch or beech branch, 150 to 200 mm in diameter and 20 mm thick, decorated with a pyrographed design in the radiating grain. The round slice surface is ideal for learning pyrography shading because the annual ring pattern guides the design layout. The technique covers the shading tip, the writing tip, and the burning sequence.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the wood slice preparation method; general pyrography technique from traditional decorative woodcraft traditions.

content.text: A wood round slice is one of the best surfaces for learning

provide a natural design grid, and the flat end-grain surface burns more evenly than face-grain boards. Birch and beech slices are the most consistent; avoid slices with large knots in the design area.

Sand the top face of the slice to 320-grit: the face must be smooth and free of saw marks, which would interfere with the tip travel. The end-grain surface sands more slowly than face-grain. Work from coarser paper if needed. Check that the slice is fully seasoned: a slice that is still damp inside will hiss under the tip and produce uneven burns.

A good first design follows the annual ring pattern: a simple mandala or botanical motif that radiates from the centre pith. Sketch the design lightly in pencil. For a 180 mm slice, a motif that fills the inner 100 mm diameter is the right scale; leave a plain border around the edge to let the natural wood show. Geometric designs (triangles, fan shapes) work well on end-grain because they can be laid out from the ring pattern directly.
Work from light to dark: always burn the palest tones first and darken them last. This is because the tip can add darkness but cannot remove it. Set the burner to a medium-low heat and use the
for the outline lines first. Use the
to fill tonal areas with a circular or hatching motion, keeping the tip moving to avoid hot spots.

The late-wood rings (the darker, harder bands in each annual ring) burn darker and more slowly than the early-wood bands. This gives end-grain pyrography a natural light-dark alternation that can be incorporated into the design deliberately. A tip moved across the grain burns through alternating light and dark bands; a tip moved with the grain produces a more even tone.

Blow away carbon dust from the surface before finishing. The slice can be left as-is for a natural look, or finished with a light coat of beeswax or Danish oil. Wipe the finish on thinly and buff off immediately; heavy coating over pyrography dims the contrast between burned and unburned areas.

attrs.fix: Keep the tip moving at a consistent pace. A slower stroke = darker burn. Practice on scrap end-grain first to calibrate tip speed at the heat setting being used.
attrs.cause: Inconsistent tip speed over the surface
attrs.symptom: The burn is uneven: some areas are very dark, others barely marked

attrs.fix: Clean the tip on the cooling stand using a folded piece of fine-grit sandpaper to remove oxidation. Increase the heat setting slightly. A well-maintained tip glides; a dirty or cool tip drags.
attrs.cause: Tip temperature too low or the tip surface oxidised
attrs.symptom: The tip drags across the surface rather than gliding

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.4 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
